title: Episode 26 On the Blue Moon
wikitext:
[]: Episode 26: On the Blue Moon             Quote Post by Director Black » Sun Oct 16, 2016 12:00 pm I consider myself a defender of the last two episode of the series. It's not perfect, but it just gather some great insight into many of the characters. However, I find myself thinking about the very final scene where everyone was congratulating Shinji. They're all on the blue moon, the same one where Shinji has his conversation with Rei as he starts to reject Instrumentality in EoE. My interpretation of the scene is that it was a fantasy of Shinji as he was talking to Rei on the moon, which explains why both areas are the same. Still, in true Eva fashion, there are many ways at looking at it, which is way I want to hear from others.

[]: Re: Episode 26: On the Blue Moon             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Oct 16, 2016 12:32 pm The last scene of EoTV specifically occurs over a nondescript oceanic world defined by nothing but coral reefs. Corals are built from thousands of individual animals called polyps, so the visual could be readily construed as an indication that HIP succeeded and humanity has become a "coral reef". Alternatively, since NGE relishes in being vague and leaving us to find our own meaning, this visual metaphor doesn't have to confirm anything beyond what we already know, that we are by default a communal organism, and Shinji is simply having his intrinsic value to his fellow polyps confirmed. You don't need to be part of a hive mind to be a productive member of society and help build something great. If you notice, the visuals in EoE show the moon, earth, sun, Black Moon, ocean, and stars all inhabiting the same space in an impossible arrangement. Shinji and Rei are in the Sea of LCL, another realm of space, and the idea being conveyed is that of being everywhere-nowhere, everything-nothing. Quite different from the end of EoTV.

[]: Re: Episode 26: On the Blue Moon             Quote Post by StrokeMeGoat » Tue Oct 25, 2016 10:27 pm I think what you refer to specifically as the blue moon, and what Reichu claims is a coral reef is more of a small, personal world that represents the smaller version of the world that exists within Shinji's own mind that they talk about a good part of the episode. He asks many times "where" he exists, and it's repeated several times that there is more than one Shinji Ikari, but the only one that exists for him is one that encompasses all of the things that make him himself, meaning all of the world he perceives is indeed himself (to include even the other people he knows, the Misato et al. in his mind are all a part of himself, and he uses them to define who he is, similarly to virtually everything else he experiences that he perceives to be something other than himself), even though it's only a narrow sliver of reality limited by sensory perception and how he chooses to interpret situations. The reason I believe this represents his own miniature world in his mind is further backed up, at least in my opinion, by the way it's exposed. Before understanding "where" he is, and who he is as a person, he's caught in a thick fog where he has no idea where it is that he exists, and suddenly he finds his place once he learns that, as he puts it, "it's okay to be here!", and suddenly the fog blows away and he's standing in the center of his own little world surrounded by the people that love him, congratulating him on learning it's okay for him to be there, that he has intrinsic self-worth (doesn't have to pilot Eva for people to need him or like him because he "isn't used to being loved" and so can't recognize that he is, because he hates himself; it would therefore be impossible for him to believe anybody else could love him either). I definitely don't understand why anybody who didn't watch these final two episodes when they were first released (or at least watched NGE for the first time after EOE was released) would say these episodes are bad or should be skipped for any reason. I always found it really strange that people would say to skip the last two episodes and just watch EOE instead. Funnily enough, the same people often complained about how negative the ending of EOE was (or at least if you perceive it to be, which I don't but a lot of people do), but also say the last two episodes suck despite being a definitively happy ending. The last two episodes just give you a much more detailed version of what happens during instrumentality before Shinji decides to tell Rei he'd like to go back to the way things were before in EOE. It makes it much clearer that the ending of EOE is actually hopeful and positive despite how terrible the situation seems. Honestly, the best thing to recommend to anybody wondering is to say to watch the series in full, just go along with the ending despite any confusion you might have, and then watch EOE, while noting that eps 25 and 26 outline what happens just before, and during instrumentality (then again, it says so itself in the episodes). It ought to be clear to anybody that watches that EOE was being planned at least to some extent when these episodes were released based on scenes they show in them that are seen in EOE. Even if they were only a few scenes Anno had come up with without a complete story and he sort of retconned the rest using the images shown during the last two eps as a springboard, it still shows EOE was planned. Honestly, it wouldn't surprise me if Anno made the last two so ambiguous (without having watched EOE) because he knew he may well get the funding for a theatrical release where he wasn't limited by his terribly constricted budget (resulting of course from the gore and violence from episode 18), and NGE could potentially get the ending it deserved. I'm sure his comments on being satisfied with the last two episodes isn't entirely false though, when I watched NGE in its entirety for the first time when I was 14, I wasn't immediately aware that End of Evangelion existed and wasn't upset with the original ending at all. It didn't make total sense, but it was very thematically relevant and I thought it fit with the show itself. A bit anticlimactic to be sure, but nothing I would call bad.

[]: Re: Episode 26: On the Blue Moon             Quote Post by Director Black » Wed Oct 26, 2016 5:56 pm StrokeMeGoat wrote: I definitely don't understand why anybody who didn't watch these final two episodes when they were first released (or at least watched NGE for the first time after EOE was released) would say these episodes are bad or should be skipped for any reason. I always found it really strange that people would say to skip the last two episodes and just watch EOE instead. Funnily enough, the same people often complained about how negative the ending of EOE was (or at least if you perceive it to be, which I don't but a lot of people do)
